sources.title: How to Start a Startup
claims: A startup is a company designed to grow fast.
Startups need to build something users actually want.
Startups should measure growth rate obsessively early on.

sources.title: Do Things that Don't Scale
claims: Startups should initially do things that don't scale.
Recruiting users manually is an acceptable early-stage tactic.
Founders should provide surprisingly good customer support early on.

sources.title: Maker's Schedule, Manager's Schedule
claims: The maker's schedule requires long uninterrupted blocks of time.
Meetings are disruptive to makers because they break up the day.
Managers operate on a schedule divided into one-hour intervals.

sources.title: How to Get Startup Ideas
claims: The best startup ideas often come from the founders' own problems.
Live in the future and notice what is missing.
Competition is a sign that an idea is worth exploring.

sources.title: Be Relentlessly Resourceful
claims: Relentless resourcefulness is the defining trait of successful founders.
Startups face problems that seem impossible until they are solved.

sources.title: What You Can't Say
claims: Societies have moral fashions just like clothing fashions.
Ideas that are taboo in one era may become accepted in another.

sources.title: The Age of the Essay
claims: The essay is a flexible form that lets the writer explore an idea.
Essays can be about any subject, not just literature.

sources.title: Why Smart People Have Bad Ideas
claims: Smart people sometimes choose ideas that are impressive but not valuable.
The best problems are ones you personally experience.

sources.title: How to Make Wealth
claims: Wealth is created by doing what people want, not by moving it around.
A startup compresses a lifetime's worth of work into a few years.
Measurement and leverage are necessary to get rich.

sources.title: Taste for Makers
claims: Good design is simple.
Good design is often slightly funny.
Good design is hard but looks easy.

sources.title: What Startups Are Really Like
claims: Startups are emotionally intense and unpredictable.
Startups die more often from denial than from competition.
Founder disputes are a common cause of startup failure.

sources.title: The Hardest Lessons for Startups to Learn
claims: Startups should release early and iterate.
Startups should not let competitors dictate their strategy.
Hiring too fast is a common and dangerous mistake.

sources.title: How to Think for Yourself
claims: Independent thinking requires both originality and domain expertise.
Most people do not think independently because it is socially costly.

sources.title: Write Simply
claims: Simple writing is more powerful than complex writing.
Jargon and abstraction often hide weak thinking.

sources.title: How to Work Hard
claims: Working hard requires both intensity and consistency over years.
It is easier to work hard on something you find genuinely interesting.

sources.title: The Lesson to Unlearn
claims: School trains students to optimize for test scores rather than real understanding.
The real world rewards solving important problems, not passing exams.

sources.title: Life is Short
claims: Life is short, and many daily activities consume more time than they appear to.
Eliminating bullshit from your life creates more time for what matters.

sources.title: Mean People Fail
claims: Mean people rarely win in the long run.
Kindness is a competitive advantage in business and life.

sources.title: Before the Startup
claims: You do not need to start a startup to be successful.
Founders should understand the risks before committing to a startup.

sources.title: Schlep Blindness
claims: People are blind to tedious problems that could be valuable startup ideas.
The best opportunities often involve work that others avoid.
